item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is assumed to be non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the original source cannot be found. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Initially I upvoted, despite thinking the immunosuppressive part should be axed and all symptoms be due to the 1086 bacteria. Then I got stuck on something and changed to no vote.

It sounds very much like each 1086-1 will pass on the disease to a separate random person when they die. If so…

Initially there were multiple people infected, and this number is never going to go down because of the transmission-by-death effect. So by only keeping one 1086-1 in custody at a time, you are just letting all the other instances be uncontained. The only way I can make sense of it is if all 1086-1s have a single person that will inherit the disease. Even then, I don't think you can reduce the number of 1086-1.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer removed. Edits implemented.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ Show whatever
- Hide whatever

Change the following:

MTF Gamma-5 ("They're on our side, sir!") was unable to completely prevent all news of the phenomenon from escaping, but was successful in creating multiple false leads and misdirecting public understanding of the event.

To this:

MTF Gamma-5 ("Red Herring") was unable to completely prevent all news of the phenomenon from escaping, but was successful in creating multiple false leads and misdirecting public understanding of the event.

This both rids the article of the error in MTF name and allows for others to read other articles featuring Gamma-5.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ok, got it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reverted because you aren't allowed to edit stuff like that without explicit permission, but feel free to send Eskobar a note about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Because the author wanted to use the old name.

Remember the phrase; "There is no canon". Authors are free to go against official site listings and MTF designations, but it can make writing a SCP that much harder.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The MTF name was changed back. May we know why? The current name is awful and does not match the actual codename.

EDIT: Since it won't change the content of the article and the author wished to match the canonical list, I'm editing. Feel free to undo it if you find a good reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Edited the code name of MTF Gamma-5 to reflect their current code name in the Task Force guide. The original ("They're on our side, sir!") was bothering me so I checked and that has not been their code name in a long time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yea, my point exactly. This anomaly is far too random. There's a very marked difference between cosmic horror and random stuff. While superficially scary (OMG it's AIDS that makes you turn into an alien and die), there's just too much random about it, and totally breaks suspension of disbelief. I have downvoted, for now.

Why is it bacterial? Viral would be far more logical… And what is the purpose of this thing? It's obviously some kind of attempt at communication, but in that case, why does it kill people? Very specifically using blood cells to build the second brain, with the additional part of returning them into a pluripotent stage, and then killing the host AND the new brain, this approach is far too specific and far too botched — if I imagine an alien intellect trying to engineer this thing with limited understanding of human anatomy (or no understanding, the infection having been engineered for a different species), this effect is like a football player missing the goalpost, but managing to kick the ball in a way that it flies into the stadium manager's office, and hits the keypad on his floor safe in a way that it enters the correct combination and unlocks it.

It just ruins the creepy for me, sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I definitely could do without the "OMG alien invasion" implication made by the discovery log and it kind of bothers me why this first iteration of -2 was required in the first place instead of just triggering autobiogenesis here and there - but even so, I have greatly enjoyed this. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So… if I understand this, it means that each 24 hours or so, another person dies of this, someone gets infected, needs to be captured, and starts over?
Is it one for each instance alive, or there was one for each infected the first time? 'cause if that's so, we're screwed…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Unless I missed it, the article never specifically states that SCP-1086 infectees are to be designated SCP-1086-1. This should probably be fixed.

Also;

SCP-1086-2 will begin sending increasingly complex signals to the brain, which affected individuals will perceive as droning music and mumbling voices.

How do we know what the infectee is perceiving if they've lost the ability to form coherent sentences?

Altogether, though, I like it. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Excellent use of real unexplained atmospheric phenomena, and what a damned creepy disease!

If there are multiple SCP-1086-1's (and I should mention, it took me until the stages of infection to realize that meant "people infected by SCP-1086"), do they all chant the same numbers (in unison? Would be extra creepy), or is it one new target for each iteration?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is during the incident described at the end, when a bunch of these fell from the sky and contaminated individuals were walking around normally (for a while) picking them up and taking pictures. Not shown: an MTF knocking this guy down and taking him in for testing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why do they have a picture of someone holding 1086-2 barehanded if it's stated in the article that exposure to the substance isn't allowed for containment reasons?

Othere than that, great article! I love the method of transferral particularly. I like the idea of the retrieval agents racing to detain the new infectee, and the infectee being suddenly dragged away by a bunch of soldiers and scientists in hazmat suits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Bacteriostatic means that it stops bacteria from reproducing. this includes, the erythromycin groups, the two medications in Bactrim/Septra, and the tetracycline group. These would probably not help very much, since this is such an aggressive infection, and the immune system is being damaged.

Bacteriocidal agents kill bacteria. These antibiotics include the penicillins, cephalosporins, fluoroquinolones, vancomycin and others. These might have some benefit. Alcohol is also bacteriocidal, as well as iodine and many others.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Edit made. And in the future, you might want to go ahead and assume that my ignorance of human immune function is a little greater than yours, if you just pulled that off the top of your head.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Antibiotics are made in a lab, so they can't read or define genetic material, which means they will attack almost anything in your body its sees as a threat.

Uh… what? =O.o=

Antibiotics work since they are substances that interfere with the lifecycle of bacteria.
There are grossly, two types of them - bacteriostatic ones, which prevent reproduction of a particular set of bacteria (example would be the erythromycin , if I recall right) or cytotoxic (? not sure if right term) ones, which outright fuck up the bacteria (if I recall right, an example would be fluoroquinolones which inhibit the function of an enzyme , gyrase, which unwinds the bacterium's DNA when it is being read)

If the infection is susceptible, high enough doses of this stuff could save at least a chunk of the patients in question. It probably won't be nowhere near 100% but some should survive if pumped with enough chemistry and isolated from further infectious agents.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To quote Yahoo Answers, echoing my understanding of immunosuppression and antibiotic treatment:

"Antibiotics are made in a lab, so they can't read or define genetic material, which means they will attack almost anything in your body its sees as a threat. The problem with this is that in some circumstances antibiotics will attack parts of the immune system weakening it even more making it worse."

Roughly: Antibiotics are made to assist a natural immune system, not replace it; they can do as much harm as good in this sort of instance. As for the sterile environment…well, I hadn't thought of that. I'll consider editing to mention that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This'd be almost awesome, however, I can't see why administering certain sorts of antibiotics wouldn't help deal with the problem, unless the infection is viral?
In theory you could keep such a person in a sterile enviroment and try to replace his blood marrow (if it works for leukaemia - wrong immune cells, it might work for none, too)

Because the rest of it is so neat, I'm not downvoting, and this might in fact be my own ignorance of the workings of human immune system showing, but whatever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Many thanks for the kind words, but I couldn't help but notice that you didn't actually upvote. Was that metaphorical support?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On 07/08/94, over two hundred small gelatinous masses fell onto the town of Oakville, WA, USA.

So, it's star jelly?

http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Star_jelly

I like it either way. Upvoted for strange, kind of creepy disease.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Hm, weird disease, yay." *keeps reading* "Oh it turns your brain INTO a jello mold of its own snot, that you hork up before you die… Nasty." *keeps reading* "Oh brain control too? Eh." *keeps reading* "Dear god kill it with fire! Wait what do you mean that makes it WORSE!?" *keeps reading*

…. +1. This is a good example (in my mind) of a bunch of semi-weak parts that end up being brought together in the right way to make a solid structure. Kinda reminded of the AVG commercial where they make a tiger cage out of toothpicks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, thanks for the timeline help. I went ahead and took out the link; I had it in there in an attempt to demonstrate exactly that the Foundation had failed in total containment, but I guess it went too far.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The article is well written, but I am not much into disease-based SCPs. No vote, then, since it's my personal taste speaking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't like the link too. It's too close to your article, making it look like Foundation failed at disinformation campaign. I think you could just drop the link, but leave the rest of the addendum intact.

Also, there are some timespan inconsistencies:

+ show block
– hide block

SCP-1086-1 instances become contagious within six hours of initial infection, giving twelve hours total of advance warning to stop a potential epidemic.

During this time, SCP-1086-1 iterations will begin uttering a name and a series of numbers;

Individuals will repeat these phrases for one hour, at which point SCP-1086-1 will go into convulsions and expel SCP-1086-2 orally.

Infected individuals will enter a comatose state and die within six (6) hours after expulsion.

Twelve hours after the death of one SCP-1086-1 iteration, the individual named during Stage 3 will spontaneously contract SCP-1086 and become symptomatic.

1 hour of repeating + 6 hours in coma before death + 12 hours before new individual infected + 6 hours before he becomes contagious = 25 hours total of advance warning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am thoroughly meh on this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Do you mean the MTF in the last addendum? That's not mine; that one's well established on the MTF page. I never liked the code name myself, but it would be silly to create a disinformation MTF when one exists for exactly that purpose.

Regarding the other comments: What exactly is wonky? I wrote this in bits and pieces, but I can't see exactly where the wonk happens. And are you saying I should just drop the link at the end?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The concept isn't bad, but the structure is a little wonky in places, the MTF's code name desperately needs to be changed, and the off-site article you link to does nothing to help your case. Downvote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, that's about it. The autobiogenesis will happen either way; leaving the infected individual alive just lets the Foundation know where the new host is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My understanding is that someone new will be infected *whenever* the last remaining host of 1086 dies-keeping it to stage three just lets them figure out who the new infectee will *be*.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The one problem I have with this SCP is that if there are only a handful of people infected at any given time, couldn't you just kill them before they start citing names and coordinates?

Otherwise, it's pretty creepy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted.

Made two small edits, one for tense and one for a common term. Whatever means are necessary -> whatever means necessary. It's debatable, but yeah.

Overall a great SCP. You didn't mention anything about how the subjects are kept alive or what effect, if any, antibiotics have on 1086. I was just curious about that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the individual named during Stage 3 will spontaneously contract SCP-1086

It's creepy enough on its own, but this made me proverbially crap myself. The fact that it can basically infect anyone, anywhere, without warning… *meep* Upvoted hard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Been working on this for a week or so, though I would put it out for further criticism. Please, if you downvote, leave some kind of comment about what isn't working for you; I want to know how to improve this.

And if you upvote, have a cookie. No, really, just go nuts.
